data.title: Is Lumentum’s Recent 90% Rally Warranted After Raised Guidance for 2025?
data.description:
If you have Lumentum Holdings on your radar, you are definitely not alone. It is one of those stocks that seems to keep investors talking, whether they are asking if it is still worth holding after a spectacular run or wondering if now is the right moment to jump in. Over the past year, the stock has soared by 134.5%, with an eye-catching 90.7% gain just since the start of this year. Even the three- and five-year returns have outperformed many peers, climbing 132.1% and 91.4%, respectively.

That being said, this ride has not always been smooth. While last week saw a solid 2.2% uptick, the stock is down by 4.5% over the past month, a reminder that market sentiment and sector trends continue to sway short-term results. Some of this optimism has been linked to broader market strength in technology and communications, but rising interest rates and shifting competitive dynamics have also played a role in shaping investor expectations.

Of course, the big question is whether the current price, now hovering at $163.23 at last close, reflects Lumentum’s true worth. Valuation is where things get interesting. Based on a straightforward scorecard measuring six different checks of undervaluation, Lumentum comes in with a value score of just 0. According to these standard methods, the company does not appear undervalued at all. But before you decide what that means for your portfolio, it is helpful to take a look at what each of these valuation methods actually says and then, at the end, explore a more nuanced approach to gauging true value.

Lumentum Holdings scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Lumentum Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is one of the most widely used methods for estimating a company's intrinsic value. It works by forecasting the company's expected future cash flows and then discounting them back to today's value using a required rate of return. In simple terms, it attempts to answer what those cash flows are worth in today's dollars.

For Lumentum Holdings, the current free cash flow stands at -$43.37 million, which means the company has recently spent more cash than it generated. However, projections indicate a more optimistic outlook. Analyst estimates cover the next five years and, for Lumentum, future free cash flow is expected to steadily grow, reaching around $534.33 million by 2028. Extending the timeline further using conservative growth assumptions, the DCF model incorporates these longer-term projections to estimate the company’s total value.

According to this model, Lumentum’s intrinsic fair value is $143.37 per share. Compared to the current trading price of $163.23, the DCF suggests the stock is about 13.9% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lumentum Holdings.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Lumentum Holdings may be overvalued by 13.9%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: Lumentum Holdings Price vs Sales

The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is a preferred valuation method for companies like Lumentum Holdings in the growth stage or those with volatile earnings, as it focuses on overall revenue instead of profits, which may fluctuate year to year. For profitable and established businesses, multiples like the P/S help investors assess whether the stock price makes sense relative to the company's top-line performance.

Normally, growth prospects and industry risk play a big role in determining what a “fair” P/S multiple should be. Companies expected to post higher revenue growth or possessing lower risk profiles often justify higher multiples, while slower-growing or riskier companies tend to receive lower ones.

Lumentum Holdings currently trades at a P/S ratio of 7.04x. For context, that is significantly above the industry average of 2.22x and the average among its peers, which sits at 4.55x. At first glance, this might suggest some level of overvaluation compared to the broader sector.

This is where Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” comes in. Unlike simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio incorporates a range of factors such as Lumentum’s expected growth, risk, profit margin, industry trends, and company size. In this case, the Fair Ratio for Lumentum is calculated at 4.08x, giving a much more customized benchmark for what is “fair” for the stock given its unique characteristics.

Comparing the current P/S of 7.04x to the Fair Ratio of 4.08x, Lumentum trades well above what would be considered reasonable after adjusting for its fundamentals and outlook. This suggests the market may be pricing in more optimism than is warranted by the company’s underlying story.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Lumentum Holdings Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is a clear, evidence-backed story about a company’s future, created by connecting your own view of how the business will perform with specific assumptions about its future revenue, earnings, and margins, ultimately calculating a fair value. Narratives bridge the gap between “just the numbers” and real-world context, letting investors see not just what a company is worth, but why.

You do not need to be a finance pro to use Narratives. Millions of investors on Simply Wall St access and create these on every company’s Community page, helping turn their research into decisions. Narratives allow you to track how your story matches the current share price, showing clearly when the market price is below or above your estimated fair value, so you know if it is time to buy, hold, or sell. In addition, these Narratives automatically update as new news and results arrive, so your perspective grows alongside the business. For example, on Lumentum Holdings, the most bullish Narrative (anchored to booming AI-related demand) estimates a fair value of around $165 per share, while the most cautious Narrative (focused on risk from key customers and supply constraints) sees fair value closer to $83. data.title: Assessing Christian Dior Shares After 16% Rally and Fresh Optimism in Global Luxury Markets
data.description:
If you have been watching Christian Dior's share price recently, you probably noticed some interesting moves. In just the past 30 days, the stock climbed 16.4%, a strong rally that might turn heads even if you have been following the broader luxury sector. Over the past week alone, Dior notched a 6.0% gain, showing renewed confidence or perhaps a change in market sentiment around the brand. That said, when we zoom out, the year-to-date return is still down 6.7%, and the one-year return sits just below zero at -0.6%. Investors who played the long game over the past five years, though, have still seen a rewarding 55.3% total return.

These price movements have been shaped by market-wide optimism toward high-end consumer brands, with Dior benefiting as luxury goods see renewed interest, likely helped by favorable trends in global demand and evolving consumer preferences. While not every blip on the chart has a news headline behind it, it's clear the perception of Dior's risk and growth potential has shifted somewhat recently.

So, does Christian Dior’s recent momentum mean the stock is undervalued, or has the market already priced in all the good news? When we look at valuation, the company scores a 3 out of 6 on our value checklist, indicating potential opportunities but also some caution. In the next section, we will break down these valuation approaches for Dior one by one, and at the end, introduce a more holistic way to think about what the company is really worth.

Why Christian Dior is lagging behind its peers

Approach 1: Christian Dior Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the intrinsic value of a company by forecasting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. This approach helps investors understand what the business is worth based on its ability to generate cash in the future.

For Christian Dior, the DCF analysis uses a two-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The company’s latest reported Free Cash Flow is an impressive €13.5 Billion, with analyst projections indicating steady growth over the coming decade. Over the next ten years, free cash flow is forecast to rise gradually, reaching around €17.7 Billion by 2035. While reliable analyst estimates typically extend only five years, longer-term figures are extrapolated to provide a longer runway for valuation.

Based on these future cash flows, the model calculates a fair value of €1,159 per share. According to this estimate, Dior’s stock is trading at a 51.6% discount. This suggests that the share price is significantly below what the company’s long-term cash generation supports.

Approach 2: Christian Dior Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a strong indicator for profitable companies like Christian Dior, as it shows how investors value the stock relative to its earnings power. For established firms with consistent profits, the PE ratio helps investors weigh current pricing against potential future earnings. This makes it a practical way to assess value beyond just short-term market moves.

A company’s PE ratio reflects not only its current profitability but also market expectations for its future growth and the level of risk investors are willing to bear. Firms with higher growth prospects or lower perceived risk tend to command higher PE ratios, while those facing uncertainty or slower growth generally trade at lower multiples.

Currently, Christian Dior trades on a PE of 22.2x. This is above the luxury sector average of 18.3x, but below the peer group average of 39.2x. In this case, Dior’s actual PE multiple is very close to its Fair Ratio. This suggests that the stock price is roughly in line with its fundamentals and expected performance.

data.title: Is GMéxico Transportes. de (BMV:GMXT) A Risky Investment?
data.description:
The external fund manager backed by Berkshire Hathaway's Charlie Munger, Li Lu, makes no bones about it when he says 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. Importantly, GMéxico Transportes, S.A.B. de C.V. (BMV:GMXT) does carry debt. But the more important question is: how much risk is that debt creating?

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

What Risk Does Debt Bring?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. If things get really bad, the lenders can take control of the business. However, a more usual (but still expensive) situation is where a company must dilute shareholders at a cheap share price simply to get debt under control. Having said that, the most common situation is where a company manages its debt reasonably well - and to its own advantage. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

How Much Debt Does GMéxico Transportes. de Carry?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at June 2025 GMéxico Transportes. de had debt of Mex$31.7b, up from Mex$27.2b in one year. However, it also had Mex$6.33b in cash, and so its net debt is Mex$25.3b.

How Strong Is GMéxico Transportes. de's Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that GMéxico Transportes. de had liabilities of Mex$15.1b due within a year, and liabilities of Mex$50.4b falling due after that. Offsetting this, it had Mex$6.33b in cash and Mex$10.2b in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities total Mex$49.0b more than the combination of its cash and short-term receivables.

With net debt sitting at just 1.1 times EBITDA, GMéxico Transportes. de is arguably pretty conservatively geared. The good news is that GMéxico Transportes. de has increased its EBIT by 4.3% over twelve months, which should ease any concerns about debt repayment. But our final consideration is also important, because a company cannot pay debt with paper profits; it needs cold hard cash. So we clearly need to look at whether that EBIT is leading to corresponding free cash flow. Over the most recent three years, GMéxico Transportes. de recorded free cash flow worth 57% of its EBIT, which is around normal, given free cash flow excludes interest and tax. This free cash flow puts the company in a good position to pay down debt, when appropriate.

The external fund manager backed by Berkshire Hathaway's Charlie Munger, Li Lu, makes no bones about it when he says 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. As with many other companies Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación, S.A.B. de C.V. (BMV:VOLARA) makes use of debt. But should shareholders be worried about its use of debt?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Generally speaking, debt only becomes a real problem when a company can't easily pay it off, either by raising capital or with its own cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more usual (but still expensive) situation is where a company must dilute shareholders at a cheap share price simply to get debt under control. Of course, the upside of debt is that it often represents cheap capital, especially when it replaces dilution in a company with the ability to reinvest at high rates of return. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

What Is Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de's Net Debt?

You can click the graphic below for the historical numbers, but it shows that as of June 2025 Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de had US$741.5m of debt, an increase on US$638.1m, over one year. But on the other hand it also has US$789.0m in cash, leading to a US$47.6m net cash position.

How Healthy Is Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de's Balance Sheet?

Zooming in on the latest balance sheet data, we can see that Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de had liabilities of US$1.94b due within 12 months and liabilities of US$3.42b due beyond that. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of US$789.0m as well as receivables valued at US$229.9m due within 12 months. So its liabilities total US$4.34b more than the combination of its cash and short-term receivables.

The deficiency here weighs heavily on the US$823.3m company itself, as if a child were struggling under the weight of an enormous back-pack full of books, his sports gear, and a trumpet. So we'd watch its balance sheet closely, without a doubt. At the end of the day, Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de would probably need a major re-capitalization if its creditors were to demand repayment. Given that Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de has more cash than debt, we're pretty confident it can handle its debt, despite the fact that it has a lot of liabilities in total.

See our latest analysis for Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de

Importantly, Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de's EBIT fell a jaw-dropping 51% in the last twelve months. If that earnings trend continues then paying off its debt will be about as easy as herding cats on to a roller coaster. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you want to see what the professionals think, you might find this free report on analyst profit forecasts to be interesting.

Finally, a business needs free cash flow to pay off debt; accounting profits just don't cut it. While Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de has net cash on its balance sheet, it's still worth taking a look at its ability to convert earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) to free cash flow, to help us understand how quickly it is building (or eroding) that cash balance. Happily for any shareholders, Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de actually produced more free cash flow than EBIT over the last three years. That sort of strong cash generation warms our hearts like a puppy in a bumblebee suit.

Summing Up

Although Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de's balance sheet isn't particularly strong, due to the total liabilities, it is clearly positive to see that it has net cash of US$47.6m. And it impressed us with free cash flow of US$422m, being 150% of its EBIT. Despite the cash, we do find Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación. de's level of total liabilities concerning, so we're not particularly comfortable with the stock. Approach 1: Globalstar Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is a widely used approach that estimates a company's intrinsic value by projecting its future free cash flows and discounting them back to their present value. This method aims to capture the value of money generated by the business, adjusted for the time value of money.

For Globalstar, current free cash flow stands at $304.44 million. According to analyst estimates and subsequent forecasts, the company's FCF is projected to fluctuate over the next decade, reaching about $203.79 million in 2035. Notably, analyst projections extend out to five years, with further estimates extrapolated beyond that period. This forward-looking model incorporates both the uncertainty and opportunity inherent in long-term forecasting for a technology-focused business.

Based on these cash flow projections, the estimated intrinsic value per share for Globalstar comes out to $34.02. However, when compared to the company’s current share price, the DCF calculation suggests the stock is trading at a 30.2% premium to its intrinsic value. For businesses still in high-growth mode or investing heavily in future opportunities, the P/S can give investors a sense of how much they are paying for each dollar of revenue, regardless of near-term profitability.

A company’s P/S ratio reflects how investors weigh future growth potential against current risks. If a firm is expected to grow rapidly, it may command a higher P/S multiple. Conversely, higher risks or subdued sales growth could justify a lower ratio. For context, Globalstar currently trades at a P/S ratio of 21.5x. data.description:
Trying to figure out what to do with O'Reilly Automotive stock? You are not alone. Whether you are debating a new position or thinking about taking profits after years of impressive returns, the decision can feel complicated. O'Reilly has certainly caught investors' attention lately; its shares are up 1.5% in the last week and they have soared a staggering 232.0% over the past five years. After shaking off a volatile month with a slight dip of 4.0%, the company's year-to-date climb of 27.8% shows just how resilient the stock has been amid shifting market sentiment toward automotive retailers.

Some of this long-term growth reflects persistent demand in the auto parts sector, as well as O'Reilly's steady execution in capturing market share. While the industry has seen its share of headwinds, O'Reilly's one-year return of 26.0% suggests that investors continue to believe in its model and future. Of course, the big question is whether the recent price levels still offer a good buying opportunity, especially given a valuation score of 0 out of 6 on key checks for undervaluation.

So, how should investors really gauge value here? Next, we will break down the classic valuation techniques, then reveal a smarter way to look at O'Reilly Automotive stock toward the end of the article.

O'Reilly Automotive scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: O'Reilly Automotive Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company's intrinsic value by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to their present value. This allows investors to see what the company might be worth today based on its expected financial performance.

For O'Reilly Automotive, the DCF analysis uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The most recent reported free cash flow is $1.87 Billion. Analysts have forecasted ongoing robust cash flow growth, with estimates climbing to $3.18 Billion by 2029. After 2029, cash flow projections are extrapolated based on expected growth trends, as detailed by Simply Wall St.

According to this DCF model, O'Reilly Automotive's intrinsic, or fair, value per share is $66.95. When compared to the current market price, this indicates the stock is trading at a premium. The DCF calculation shows O'Reilly shares are 51.1% overvalued based solely on forward cash flow expectations.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests O'Reilly Automotive may be overvalued by 51.1%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: O'Reilly Automotive Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is widely considered the go-to metric for valuing profitable companies like O'Reilly Automotive. It tells investors how much they are paying for each dollar of current earnings, making it especially relevant for businesses with established profitability and a track record of earnings growth.

The "right" or "fair" PE ratio is not set in stone. Higher growth expectations can justify a higher multiple, while increased uncertainty or risk should push it lower. Factors such as industry trends, earnings stability, and outlook for future profit growth all play an important role in where a company's PE should land in relation to the market and its peers.

Currently, O'Reilly Automotive trades at a PE ratio of 35.4x. That is above the Specialty Retail industry average of 16.1x, and also higher than the average for its direct peers at 33.9x. Simply Wall St's proprietary Fair Ratio for O'Reilly, which is based on its future earnings potential, market cap, profit margin, and risk profile, stands at 19.4x. This is a more holistic benchmark, as it factors in company-specific growth, risks, and profitability, rather than relying solely on industry or peer averages, which can miss the unique qualities of the business.

With O'Reilly's actual PE ratio well above this Fair Ratio, the data signals the stock is overvalued when assessed through the lens of long-term earnings fundamentals.

data.title: Investors Should Be Encouraged By China Hongqiao Group's (HKG:1378) Returns On Capital
data.description:
What trends should we look for it we want to identify stocks that can multiply in value over the long term? Ideally, a business will show two trends; firstly a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and secondly, an increasing amount of capital employed. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. With that in mind, the ROCE of China Hongqiao Group (HKG:1378) looks great, so lets see what the trend can tell us.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. The formula for this calculation on China Hongqiao Group is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.26 = CN¥39b ÷ (CN¥228b - CN¥80b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to June 2025).

Therefore, China Hongqiao Group has an ROCE of 26%. In absolute terms that's a great return and it's even better than the Metals and Mining industry average of 10%.

View our latest analysis for China Hongqiao Group

In the above chart we have measured China Hongqiao Group's prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for China Hongqiao Group .

So How Is China Hongqiao Group's ROCE Trending?

The trends we've noticed at China Hongqiao Group are quite reassuring. The numbers show that in the last five years, the returns generated on capital employed have grown considerably to 26%. The amount of capital employed has increased too, by 56%. The increasing returns on a growing amount of capital is common amongst multi-baggers and that's why we're impressed.

In another part of our analysis, we noticed that the company's ratio of current liabilities to total assets decreased to 35%, which broadly means the business is relying less on its suppliers or short-term creditors to fund its operations. So this improvement in ROCE has come from the business' underlying economics, which is great to see.

In Conclusion...

All in all, it's terrific to see that China Hongqiao Group is reaping the rewards from prior investments and is growing its capital base. Since the stock has returned a staggering 618% to shareholders over the last five years, it looks like investors are recognizing these changes. With that being said, we still think the promising fundamentals mean the company deserves some further due diligence.

While China Hongqiao Group looks impressive, no company is worth an infinite price. The intrinsic value infographic for 1378 helps visualize whether it is currently trading for a fair price.
